draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational, approachable copy over formal business jargon in their CRM interface
facade.label: Friendly tone, casual language
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warm, conversational microcopy throughout the product; use contractions and casual language in onboarding, empty states, and error messages
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt formal, polished, professional language; position the CRM as a serious business tool with corporate-grade tone

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to interface elements that feel human and approachable rather than cold and corporate
facade.label: Warm sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use warmer color palettes, softer interactions, and humanized microcopy to build trust with solo workers
facade.rejectImplies: Freelancers prefer a more clinical, neutral aesthetic that emphasizes professionalism and distance over intimacy

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer approachable, non-threatening UI that feels inviting rather than corporate or harsh
facade.label: Soft, rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should use rounded buttons, card-based layouts, and gentle curves to feel welcoming and less intimidating
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM might benefit from crisp edges, geometric precision, or a more structured, no-nonsense aesthetic that signals professionalism and efficiency

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational, approachable copy over formal business jargon in their CRM interface
evidence.implication: Lean into warm, conversational microcopy throughout the product; use contractions and casual language in onboarding, empty states, and error messages

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer fluid, animated transitions over instant state changes when navigating between clients and projects—especially in mobile contexts where gesture feels more natural than clicks.
facade.label: Smooth slide-out sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should prioritize motion-driven UI: slide drawers, fade overlays, animated list reordering, gesture-based actions rather than button clicks.
facade.rejectImplies: The CRM should favor instant, snappy state changes: instant panel swaps, no-delay clicks, minimal motion—emphasizing efficiency over feel.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer a persistent navigation sidebar (for clients, projects, invoices) paired with a card-based content area to quickly scan multiple small items without scrolling.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation persistence vs. tab-based switching; card density vs. list density
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore multi-column card layouts, persistent sidebar patterns, and minimal scrolling contexts. Likely user needs: quick context switching between clients and fast visual scanning.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore tab-based top navigation or full-width list views. May indicate preference for cleaner simplicity, less cognitive load from persistent UI, or need for focused single-task workflows.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prioritize visual identity over dense contact tables—showing client face/brand builds relationship recall faster than rows of text fields
facade.axisTargeted: Visual identity (photos/avatars) vs. text-only contact data
facade.label: Client cards with photos
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into card-based layouts with imagery; client profiles should lead with visual + name; build trust through faces/logos over pure data density
facade.rejectImplies: Keep visual minimal; prioritize compact table views or text-first formats; photos feel like bloat for a work tool; focus on searchability and info density over visual warmth

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers find visual personality in notifications (emoji, icons) more engaging and less anxiety-inducing than sterile warning/success states
facade.label: Playful emoji alerts
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into light visual personality in system feedback; use emoji, illustrated icons, and playful language in alerts, notifications, and status messages
facade.rejectImplies: Stick with minimal, text-based or symbol-only notifications; prioritize clarity and professional restraint over personality in error/success states

axes.label: Whether they prefer minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a distraction-free workspace or one that feels more dynamic and branded
Whether they favor sidebar/list-based navigation vs. card/grid layouts—reveals how they want to organize and access client/project information at a glance
Whether they respond to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business tool that feels corporate or one that feels like a supportive partner
Whether they prefer calm, spacious layouts vs. information-dense, action-oriented layouts—reveals if they want a peaceful tool or one that energizes productivity
Whether they want data presented as simple summaries vs. detailed breakdowns with charts/metrics—reveals how much visibility into numbers and analytics matters to their workflow
Whether they expect instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals if they value speed and responsiveness or prefer a more thoughtful, guided experience
